## Summary
Neural correlates of consciousness (NCCs) are the minimal neuronal mechanisms jointly sufficient for any one specific conscious percept. Decades of neuroimaging and lesion studies have identified posterior cortical "hot zones" as primary NCC candidates, with the role of prefrontal cortex remaining contentious.

## Evidence
No-report paradigms (which avoid confounding consciousness with reportability) implicate posterior parietal and temporal cortices. The adversarial collaboration between IIT and GWT (2023) found evidence favoring posterior cortical involvement but was inconclusive on prefrontal necessity. Temporo-parietal junction damage selectively impairs conscious perception while sparing unconscious processing.

## Significance
Identifying NCCs is essential for clinical applications (detecting consciousness in brain-injured patients) and for adjudicating between theories. The shift toward no-report paradigms has refined the search by distinguishing correlates of consciousness from correlates of reporting.
